    32→# Why This Workflow Exists {#sec-why}

    34→## The Problem
    35→
    36→If you've ever built lecture slides, you know the pain:

    39→- **Quality is inconsistent.** One slide has perfect spacing; the next overflows. Citations compile in Overleaf but break locally. Figures look great on your screen but pixelated on a projector.

    41→- **No one checks the math.** Grammar checkers catch "teh" but not a flipped sign in a decomposition theorem.
    42→
    43→This workflow solves all of these problems. You describe what you want --- "translate Lecture 5 to Quarto" or "create a new lecture on synthetic control" --- and Claude handles the rest: plans the approach, implements it, runs specialized reviewers, fixes issues, verifies quality, and presents results. Like a contractor who manages the entire job.

    45→## What Makes Claude Code Different
    46→
    47→Claude Code runs on your computer with full access to your file system, terminal, and git. It works as a **CLI tool**, a **VS Code extension**, or through the **Claude Desktop app** --- same capabilities, same configuration, different interface. Here is what that enables:

    62→This workflow was developed over 6+ sessions building a PhD course on Causal Panel Data. The result: 6 complete lectures (140+ slides each), with Beamer + Quarto versions, interactive Plotly charts, TikZ diagrams, and R replication scripts --- all managed by the orchestrator and reviewed by 10 specialized agents across 5 quality dimensions.

   222→## Requirements Specification (For Complex Tasks) {#sec-spec-then-plan}

   226→::: {.panel-tabset}

   257→
   258→Claude: "Let me clarify:
   259→1. What's the pain point (speed, readability, bugs)?
   260→2. Breaking changes okay or preserve compatibility?
   261→3. Testing infrastructure available?

   268→

   277→
   278→---
   279→
   280→# The System in Action {#sec-system}
   281→
   282→With setup covered, here is what the system actually *does*. This section walks through the three core mechanisms that make the workflow powerful: specialized agents, adversarial QA, and automatic quality scoring.
   283→
   284→## Why Specialized Agents Beat One-Size-Fits-All
   285→
   286→Consider proofreading a 140-slide lecture deck. You could ask Claude:
   287→
   288→> "Review these slides for grammar, layout, math correctness, code quality, and pedagogical flow."
   289→
   290→Claude will skim everything and catch some issues. But it will miss:
   291→
   292→- The equation on slide 42 where a subscript changed from $m_t^{d=0}$ to $m_t^0$
   293→- The TikZ diagram where two labels overlap at presentation resolution
   294→- The R script that uses `k=10` covariates but the slide says `k=5`
   295→
   296→Now compare with specialized agents:
   297→
   298→| Agent | Focus | What It Catches |
   299→|-------|-------|-----------------|
   300→| `proofreader` | Grammar only | "principle" vs "principal" |
   301→| `slide-auditor` | Layout only | Text overflow on slide 37 |
   302→| `pedagogy-reviewer` | Flow only | Missing framing sentence before Theorem 3.1 |
   303→| `r-reviewer` | Code only | Missing `set.seed()` |
   304→| `domain-reviewer` | Substance | Slide says 10,000 MC reps, code runs 1,000 |
   305→
   306→Each agent reads the same file but examines a different dimension with full attention. The `/slide-excellence` skill runs them all in parallel.
   307→
   308→## The Adversarial Pattern: Critic + Fixer
   309→
   310→The single most powerful pattern in this system is the **adversarial QA loop**:
   311→
   312→```
   313→+------------------+
   314→|  quarto-critic   |  "I found 12 issues. 3 Critical."
   315→|  (READ-ONLY)     |
   316→+--------+---------+
   317→         |
   318→    +----v----+
   319→    | Verdict |
   320→    +----+----+
   321→     /       \
   322→APPROVED   NEEDS WORK
   323→    |          |
   324→  Done    +----v---------+
   325→          | quarto-fixer |  "Fixed 12/12 issues."
   326→          | (READ-WRITE) |
   327→          +----+---------+
   328→               |
   329→          +----v----------+
   330→          | quarto-critic |  "Re-audit: 2 remaining."
   331→          | (Round 2)     |
   332→          +----+----------+
   333→               |
   334→          ... (up to 5 rounds)
   335→```
   336→
   337→**Why it works:** The critic can't fix files (read-only), so it has no incentive to downplay issues. The fixer can't approve itself (the critic re-audits). This prevents the common failure of Claude saying "looks good" about its own work.
   338→
   339→::: {.callout-tip}
   340→## Real Example
   341→
   342→In Econ 730 Lecture 6, the critic caught that the Quarto version used `\cdots` (a placeholder) where the Beamer version had the full Hajek weight formula. The fixer replaced it. On re-audit, the critic found 8 more instances of missing `(X)` arguments on outcome models. After 4 rounds, the Quarto slides matched the Beamer source exactly.
   343→:::
   344→
   345→## The Orchestrator: Coordinating Agents Automatically
   346→
   347→Individual agents are specialists. Skills like `/slide-excellence` and `/qa-quarto` coordinate a few agents for specific tasks. But in day-to-day work, you should not have to think about which agents to run. That is the orchestrator's job.
   348→
   349→The **orchestrator protocol** (`.claude/rules/orchestrator-protocol.md`) is an auto-loaded rule that activates after any plan is approved. It implements the plan, runs the verifier, selects review agents based on file types, applies fixes, re-verifies, and scores against quality gates. It loops until the score meets threshold or max rounds are exhausted.
   350→
   351→You never invoke the orchestrator manually --- it is the default mode of operation for any non-trivial task. Skills remain available for standalone use (e.g., `/proofread` for a quick grammar check), but the orchestrator handles the full lifecycle automatically. See [Pattern 2](#pattern-2-contractor-mode-orchestrator) for the complete workflow.
   352→
   353→## Quality Scoring: The 80/90/95 System {#sec-quality}
   354→
   355→Every file gets a quality score from 0 to 100:
   356→
   357→| Score | Threshold | Meaning | Action |
   358→|-------|-----------|---------|--------|
   359→| **80+** | Commit | Safe to save progress | `git commit` allowed |
   360→| **90+** | PR | Ready for deployment | `gh pr create` encouraged |
   361→| **95+** | Excellence | Exceptional quality | Aspirational target |
   362→| **< 80** | Blocked | Critical issues exist | Must fix before committing |
   363→
   364→### How Scores Are Calculated
   365→
   366→Points are deducted for issues:
   367→
   368→| Issue | Deduction | Why Critical |
   369→|-------|-----------|-------------|
   370→| Equation overflow | -20 | Math cut off = unusable |
   371→| Broken citation | -15 | Academic integrity |
   372→| Equation typo | -10 | Teaches wrong content |
   373→| Text overflow | -5 | Content cut off |
   374→| Label overlap | -5 | Diagram illegible |
   375→| Notation inconsistency | -3 | Student confusion |
   376→
   377→### Mandatory Verification
   378→
   379→The verification protocol (`.claude/rules/verification-protocol.md`) requires that Claude compile, render, or otherwise verify every output before reporting a task as complete. The orchestrator enforces this as an explicit step in its loop (Step 2: VERIFY). This means Claude **cannot** say "done" without actually checking the output.
   380→
   381→::: {.callout-warning}
   382→## Don't Skip Verification
   383→
   384→In Econ 730, verification caught unverified TikZ diagrams that would have deployed with overlapping labels, broken SVGs in Quarto slides that wouldn't display, and R scripts with missing intercept terms that produced silently wrong estimates.
   385→:::
   386→
   387→## Creating Your Own Domain Reviewer
   388→
   389→The template includes `domain-reviewer.md` --- a skeleton for building a substance reviewer specific to your field.
   390→
   391→### The 5-Lens Framework
   392→
   393→Every domain can benefit from these five review lenses:
   394→
   395→| Lens | What It Checks | Example (Economics) | Example (Physics) |
   396→|------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|
   397→| **Assumption Audit** | Are stated assumptions sufficient? | Is overlap required for ATT? | Is the adiabatic approximation valid here? |
   398→| **Derivation Check** | Does the math check out? | Do decomposition terms sum? | Do the units balance? |
   399→| **Citation Fidelity** | Do slides match cited papers? | Is the theorem from the right paper? | Is the experimental setup correctly described? |
   400→| **Code-Theory Alignment** | Does code implement the formula? | R script matches the slide equation? | Simulation parameters match theory? |
   401→| **Logic Chain** | Does the reasoning flow? | Can a PhD student follow backwards? | Are prerequisites established? |
   402→
   403→To customize, open `.claude/agents/domain-reviewer.md` and fill in:
   404→
   405→1. Your domain's common assumption types
   406→2. Typical derivation patterns to verify
   407→3. Key papers and their correct attributions
   408→4. Code-theory alignment checks for your tools
   409→5. Logic chain requirements for your audience
   410→
   411→---
   412→
   413→# The Building Blocks {#sec-blocks}
   414→
   415→Understanding the configuration layers helps you customize the workflow and debug when things go wrong. Claude Code's power comes from five configuration layers that work together --- think of them as the operating system for your academic project.
   416→
   417→## CLAUDE.md --- Your Project's Constitution
   418→
   419→`CLAUDE.md` is the single most important file. Claude reads it at the start of every session. But here is the critical insight: **Claude reliably follows about 100--150 custom instructions.** Your system prompt already uses ~50, leaving ~100--150 for your project. CLAUDE.md and always-on rules share this budget.
   420→
   421→This means CLAUDE.md should be a **slim constitution** --- short directives and pointers, not comprehensive documentation. Aim for ~120 lines:
   422→
   423→- **Core principles** --- 4--5 bullets (plan-first, verify-after, quality gates, LEARN tags)
   424→- **Folder structure** --- where everything lives
   425→- **Commands** --- compilation, deployment, key tools
   426→- **Customization tables** --- Beamer environments, CSS classes
   427→- **Current state** --- what's done, what's in progress
   428→- **Skill quick reference** --- table of available slash commands
   429→
   430→Move everything else into `.claude/rules/` files (with path-scoping so they only load when relevant).
   431→
   432→```markdown
   433→# CLAUDE.MD --- My Course Development
   434→
   435→**Project:** Econ 730 --- Causal Panel Data
   436→**Institution:** Emory University
   437→
   438→## Core Principles
   439→1. **Plan-first** — enter plan mode before non-trivial tasks
   440→2. **Verify-after** — compile/render and check before reporting done
   441→3. **Quality gates** — 80 to commit, 90 for PR, 95 for excellence
   442→4. **LEARN tags** — persist corrections in MEMORY.md
   443→5. **Single source of truth** — Beamer is authoritative; derive, don't duplicate
   444→
   445→## Quick Reference
   446→| Command | What It Does |
   447→|---------|-------------|
   448→| `/compile-latex [file]` | 3-pass XeLaTeX compilation |
   449→| `/proofread [file]` | Grammar/typo review |
   450→| `/deploy [Lecture]` | Render and deploy to GitHub Pages |
   451→```
   452→
   453→::: {.callout-important}
   454→## Keep It Lean
   455→
   456→CLAUDE.md loads every session. If it exceeds ~150 lines, Claude starts ignoring rules silently. Put detailed standards in path-scoped rules (`.claude/rules/`) instead --- they only load when Claude works on matching files, so they don't compete for attention.
   457→:::
   458→
   459→## Rules --- Domain Knowledge That Auto-Loads
   460→
   461→Rules are markdown files in `.claude/rules/` that Claude loads automatically. They encode your project's standards. The key design principle is **path-scoping**: rules with a `paths:` YAML frontmatter only load when Claude works on matching files.
   462→
   463→**Always-on rules** (no `paths:` frontmatter) load every session. Keep these few and lean:
   464→
   465→```
   466→.claude/rules/
   467→├── plan-first-workflow.md       # ~35 lines — plan before you build
   468→├── orchestrator-protocol.md     # ~40 lines — contractor mode loop
   469→└── session-logging.md           # ~22 lines — three logging triggers
   470→```
   471→
   472→**Path-scoped rules** load only when relevant:
   473→
   474→```
   475→.claude/rules/
   476→├── r-code-conventions.md        # paths: ["**/*.R"] — R standards
   477→├── quality-gates.md             # paths: ["*.tex", "*.qmd", "*.R"] — scoring
   478→├── verification-protocol.md     # paths: ["*.tex", "*.qmd", "docs/"] — verify before done
   479→├── replication-protocol.md      # paths: ["scripts/**/*.R"] — replicate first
   480→├── exploration-folder-protocol.md  # paths: ["explorations/**"] — sandbox rules
   481→├── orchestrator-research.md     # paths: ["scripts/**/*.R", "explorations/**"] — simple loop
   482→└── ...14 path-scoped rules total
   483→```
   484→
   485→This design keeps always-on context under ~100 lines while providing rich, domain-specific guidance exactly when Claude needs it.
   486→
   487→**Why rules matter:** Without them, Claude will use generic defaults. With them, Claude follows *your* standards consistently across sessions.
   488→
   489→### Example: Path-Scoped R Code Conventions Rule
   490→
   491→```yaml
   492→---
   493→paths:
   494→  - "**/*.R"
   495→  - "Figures/**/*.R"
   496→  - "scripts/**/*.R"
   497→---
   498→```
   499→
   500→```markdown
   501→# R Code Standards
   502→
   503→## Reproducibility
   504→- set.seed() called ONCE at top (YYYYMMDD format)
   505→- All packages loaded at top via library()
   506→- All paths relative to repository root
   507→
   508→## Visual Identity
   509→primary_blue  <- "#012169"
   510→primary_gold  <- "#f2a900"
   511→```
   512→
   513→The `paths:` block means this rule only loads when Claude reads or edits an `.R` file. When Claude works on a `.tex` file, this rule doesn't consume any of the instruction budget.
   514→
   515→## Constitutional Governance (Optional)
   516→
   517→As your project grows, some decisions become non-negotiable (to maintain quality, reproducibility, or collaboration standards). Others remain flexible.
   518→
   519→The `templates/constitutional-governance.md` template helps you distinguish between:
   520→
   521→- **Immutable principles** (Articles I-V): Non-negotiable rules that ensure consistency
   522→- **User preferences**: Flexible patterns that can vary by context
   523→
   524→### Example Articles You Might Define
   525→
   526→- **Article I: Primary Artifact** — Which file is authoritative (e.g., `.tex` vs `.qmd`, `.Rmd` vs `.html`, notebook vs script)
   527→- **Article II: Plan-First Threshold** — When to enter plan mode (e.g., >3 files, >30 min, multi-step workflows)
   528→- **Article III: Quality Gate** — Minimum score to commit (e.g., 80/100, all tests passing)
   529→- **Article IV: Verification Standard** — What must pass before commit (e.g., compile, tests, render)
   530→- **Article V: File Organization** — Where different file types live (prevents scattering)
   531→
   532→The template includes examples for LaTeX, R, Python, Jupyter, and multi-language workflows.
   533→
   534→::: {.callout-note}
   535→## When to Use Constitutional Governance
   536→
   537→Use it **after** you've established 3-7 recurring patterns that you want to enforce consistently. Don't create it on day one — let patterns emerge first, then codify them.
   538→
   539→**When to skip:** Solo projects with evolving standards, or when you prefer case-by-case decisions.
   540→:::
   541→
   542→**Template:** `templates/constitutional-governance.md`
   543→
   544→## Skills --- Reusable Slash Commands
   545→
   546→Skills are multi-step workflows invoked with `/command`. Each skill lives in `.claude/skills/[name]/SKILL.md`:
   547→
   548→```yaml
   549→---
   550→name: compile-latex
   551→description: Compile LaTeX with 3-pass XeLaTeX + bibtex
   552→disable-model-invocation: true
   553→argument-hint: "[filename without .tex extension]"
   554→---
   555→
   556→# Steps:
   557→1. cd to Slides/
   558→2. Run xelatex pass 1
   559→3. Run bibtex
   560→4. Run xelatex pass 2
   561→5. Run xelatex pass 3
   562→6. Check for errors
   563→7. Report results
   564→```
   565→
   566→**Skills you get in the template:**
   567→
   568→| Skill | Purpose | When to Use |
   569→|-------|---------|------------|
   570→| `/compile-latex` | Build PDF from .tex | After any Beamer edit |
   571→| `/deploy` | Render Quarto + sync to docs/ | Before pushing to GitHub Pages |
   572→| `/proofread` | Grammar and consistency check | Before every commit |
   573→| `/qa-quarto` | Adversarial Quarto QA | After translating Beamer to Quarto |
   574→| `/slide-excellence` | Full multi-agent review | Before major milestones |
   575→| `/create-lecture` | New lecture from scratch | Starting a new topic |
   576→| `/commit` | Stage, commit, PR, merge | After any completed task |
   577→
   578→## Agents --- Specialized Reviewers
   579→
   580→Agents are the real power of this system. Each agent is an expert in one dimension of quality:
   581→
   582→```
   583→.claude/agents/
   584→+-- proofreader.md        # Grammar, typos, consistency
   585→+-- slide-auditor.md      # Visual layout, overflow, spacing
   586→+-- pedagogy-reviewer.md  # Narrative arc, notation clarity, pacing
   587→+-- r-reviewer.md         # R code quality and reproducibility
   588→+-- tikz-reviewer.md      # TikZ diagram visual quality
   589→+-- quarto-critic.md      # Adversarial Quarto vs Beamer comparison
   590→+-- quarto-fixer.md       # Applies critic's fixes
   591→+-- beamer-translator.md  # Beamer -> Quarto translation
   592→+-- verifier.md           # Task completion verification
   593→+-- domain-reviewer.md    # YOUR domain-specific substance review
   594→```
   595→
   596→### Agent Anatomy
   597→
   598→Each agent file has YAML frontmatter + detailed instructions:
   599→
   600→```markdown
   601→---
   602→name: proofreader
   603→description: Reviews slides for grammar, typos, and consistency
   604→---
   605→
   606→# Proofreader Agent
   607→
   608→## Role
   609→You are an expert academic proofreader reviewing lecture slides.
   610→
   611→## What to Check
   612→1. Grammar and spelling errors
   613→2. Inconsistent notation
   614→3. Missing or broken citations
   615→4. Content overflow (text exceeding slide bounds)
   616→
   617→## Report Format
   618→Save findings to: quality_reports/[FILENAME]_report.md
   619→
   620→## Severity Levels
   621→- **Critical:** Math errors, broken citations
   622→- **Major:** Grammar errors, overflow
   623→- **Minor:** Style inconsistencies
   624→```
   625→
   626→::: {.callout-note}
   627→## Why Specialized Agents?
   628→
   629→A single Claude prompt trying to check grammar, layout, math, and code simultaneously will do a mediocre job at all of them. Specialized agents focus on one dimension and do it thoroughly. The `/slide-excellence` skill runs them all in parallel, then synthesizes results.
   630→:::
   631→
   632→### Multi-Model Strategy: Cost vs. Quality
   633→
   634→Not all agents need the same model. Each agent file has a `model:` field in its YAML frontmatter. By default, all agents use `model: inherit` (they use whatever model your main session runs). But you can customize this to optimize cost:
   635→
   636→| Task Type | Recommended Model | Why | Examples |
   637→|-----------|-------------------|-----|----------|
   638→| Complex translation | `model: opus` | Needs deep understanding of both formats | beamer-translator, quarto-critic |
   639→| Fast, constrained work | `model: sonnet` | Speed matters more than depth | r-reviewer, quarto-fixer |
   640→| Default | `model: inherit` | Uses whatever the main session runs | proofreader, slide-auditor |
   641→
   642→**The principle:** Use Opus for tasks that require holding two large documents in mind simultaneously (translation, adversarial comparison). Use Sonnet for tasks with clear, bounded scope (fix these 12 issues, check this R script). Let everything else inherit.
   643→
   644→To change an agent's model, edit its YAML frontmatter:
   645→
   646→```yaml
   647→---
   648→name: quarto-critic
   649→model: opus   # was: inherit
   650→---
   651→```
   652→
   653→::: {.callout-tip}
   654→## Cost Savings
   655→
   656→If you configure model-per-agent, a typical Beamer-to-Quarto translation runs the critic on Opus (2--4 rounds) while the fixer runs on Sonnet (same rounds). This can save roughly 40--60% compared to running everything on Opus, with no quality loss on the fixing step.
   657→:::
   658→
   659→## Settings --- Permissions and Hooks
   660→
   661→`.claude/settings.json` controls what Claude is allowed to do. Here is a simplified excerpt --- the template includes additional permission entries for git, GitHub CLI, PDF tools, and more:
   662→
   663→```json
   664→{
   665→  "permissions": {
   666→    "allow": [
   667→      "Bash(git status *)",
   668→      "Bash(xelatex *)",
   669→      "Bash(Rscript *)",
   670→      "Bash(quarto render *)",
   671→      "Bash(./scripts/sync_to_docs.sh *)"
   672→    ]
   673→  },
   674→  "hooks": {
   675→    "Stop": [
   676→      {
   677→        "hooks": [{
   678→          "type": "command",
   679→          "command": "python3 \"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/log-reminder.py",
   680→          "timeout": 10
   681→        }]
   682→      }
   683→    ]
   684→  }
   685→}
   686→```
   687→
   688→The **Stop hook** runs a fast Python script after every response. No LLM call, no latency. It checks whether the session log is current and reminds Claude to update it if not. Behavioral rules like verification and Beamer-Quarto sync are enforced via auto-loaded rules in `.claude/rules/`, which is the right tool for nuanced judgment that Claude can evaluate in-context.
   689→
   690→## Memory --- Cross-Session Persistence
   691→
   692→Claude Code has an auto-memory system at `~/.claude/projects/[project]/memory/MEMORY.md`. This file persists across sessions and is loaded into every conversation.
   693→
   694→Use it for:
   695→- Key project facts that never change
   696→- Corrections you don't want repeated (`[LEARN:tag]` format)
   697→- Current plan status
   698→
   699→```markdown
   700→# Auto Memory
   701→
   702→## Key Facts
   703→- Project uses XeLaTeX, not pdflatex
   704→- Bibliography file: Bibliography_base.bib
   705→
   706→## Corrections Log
   707→- [LEARN:r-code] Package X drops obs silently when covariate is missing
   708→- [LEARN:citation] Post-LASSO is Belloni (2013), NOT Belloni (2014)
   709→- [LEARN:workflow] Every Beamer edit must auto-sync to Quarto
   710→```
   711→
   712→### Plans --- Compression-Resistant Task Memory
   713→
   714→While MEMORY.md stores long-lived project facts, **plans** store task-specific strategy. Every non-trivial plan is saved to `quality_reports/plans/` with a timestamp. This means:
   715→
   716→- Plans survive auto-compression (they are on disk, not just in context)
   717→- Plans survive session boundaries (readable in any future session)
   718→- Plans create an audit trail of design decisions
   719→
   720→See Pattern 1 in [Workflow Patterns](#sec-patterns) for the full protocol.
   721→
   722→### Session Logs --- Why-Not-Just-What History (with Automated Reminders)
   723→
   724→Git commits record what changed, but not *why*. Session logs fill this gap. Claude writes to `quality_reports/session_logs/` at three points: right after plan approval, incrementally during implementation (as decisions happen), and at session end. This means the log captures reasoning *as it happens*, before auto-compression can discard it.
   725→
   726→Because relying on instructions alone is fragile (Claude forgets during long sessions), a **Stop hook** (`.claude/hooks/log-reminder.py`) fires after every response. It tracks how many responses have passed since the session log was last updated. After a threshold, it blocks Claude from stopping until the log is current. This turns a best practice into an enforced behavior.
   727→
   728→New sessions can read these logs to understand not just the current state of the project, but the reasoning behind it. See Pattern 1 in [Workflow Patterns](#sec-patterns) for the full protocol.
   729→
   730→### How It All Fits Together
   731→
   732→With CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, plans, and session logs, the system has four distinct memory layers. Here is what each one does and when it matters:
   733→
   734→| Layer | File | Survives Compression? | Updated When | Purpose |
   735→|-------|------|----------------------|--------------|---------|
   736→| Project context | `CLAUDE.md` | Yes (on disk) | Rarely | Project rules, folder structure, commands |
   737→| Corrections | `MEMORY.md` | Yes (on disk) | On `[LEARN]` tag | Prevent repeating past mistakes |
   738→| Task strategy | `quality_reports/plans/` | Yes (on disk) | Once per task | Plan survives planning-to-implementation handoff |
   739→| Decision reasoning | `quality_reports/session_logs/` | Yes (on disk) | Incrementally | Record *why* decisions were made |
   740→| Conversation | Claude's context window | **No** (compressed) | Every response | Current working memory |
   741→
   742→The first four layers are your safety net. Anything written to disk survives indefinitely. The conversation context is ephemeral --- auto-compression will eventually discard details. The workflow's design ensures that anything worth keeping is written to one of the four persistent layers before compression can erase it.
   743→
   744→### Hooks --- Automated Enforcement
   745→
   746→The session log reminder above is one example of a broader pattern: using **hooks** to enforce rules that Claude might otherwise forget during long sessions. Rules live in context and can be compressed away. Hooks live in `.claude/settings.json` and fire every time, regardless of context state.
   747→
   748→The template includes hooks for logging, notifications, file protection, and context survival:
   749→
   750→| Hook | Event | What It Does |
   751→|------|-------|-------------|
   752→| Session log reminder | `Stop` | Reminds about session logs after every response |
   753→| Desktop notification | `Notification` | macOS alert when Claude needs attention |
   754→| File protection | `PreToolUse` | Blocks accidental edits to bibliography and settings |
   755→| Context state capture | `PreCompact` | Saves plan state before auto-compaction |
   756→| Context restoration | `SessionStart[compact|resume]` | Restores context after compaction or resume |
   757→| Context monitor | `PostToolUse[Bash|Task]` | Progressive warnings at 40%/55%/65%/80%/90% context |
   758→| Verification reminder | `PostToolUse[Write|Edit]` | Reminds to compile/render before marking done |
   759→
   760→Verification and Beamer-Quarto sync are enforced via auto-loaded rules, which are the right tool for nuanced judgment. Hooks are reserved for enforcement that *must* survive context compression.
   761→
   762→::: {.callout-tip}
   763→## Hook Design Principle
   764→Use **command-based hooks** for fast, mechanical checks (file exists? counter threshold?). Use **rules** for nuanced judgment (did Claude verify correctly?). Avoid prompt-based hooks that trigger an LLM call on every response --- the latency adds up fast.
   765→:::
   766→
   767→### Context Survival System (Advanced) {#context-survival-system-advanced}
   768→
   769→When context compaction happens, Claude loses working memory. The **context survival system** ensures you can recover seamlessly.
   770→
   771→#### How It Works
   772→
   773→Two hooks work together to preserve and restore state:
   774→
   775→```
   776→Session running → context fills up → PreCompact fires
   777→                                           ↓
   778→                                    pre-compact.py saves:
   779→                                    • Active plan path
   780→                                    • Current task
   781→                                    • Recent decisions
   782→                                           ↓
   783→                                    Auto-compaction happens
   784→                                           ↓
   785→                                    SessionStart(compact|resume) fires
   786→                                           ↓
   787→                                    post-compact-restore.py:
   788→                                    • Reads saved state
   789→                                    • Prints context summary
   790→                                    • Claude knows where it left off
   791→```
   792→
   793→#### What Gets Saved
   794→
   795→| State | Location | Purpose |
   796→|-------|----------|---------|
   797→| Plan path | Session cache | So Claude can read the plan file |
   798→| Current task | Session cache | First unchecked `- [ ]` item |
   799→| Recent decisions | Session cache | Last 3 decision-like entries from session log |
   800→| Compaction note | Session log | Timestamp marker for reference |
   801→
   802→#### Context Monitoring
   803→
   804→The `context-monitor.py` hook tracks approximate context usage and provides progressive warnings:
   805→
   806→| Threshold | Message | Purpose |
   807→|-----------|---------|---------|
   808→| 40%, 55%, 65% | Suggest `/learn` | Capture non-obvious discoveries before compaction |
   809→| 80% | Info message | Auto-compact approaching, no rush |
   810→| 90% | Caution | Complete current task with full quality |
   811→
   812→Use `/context-status` to check current session health at any time.
   813→
   814→::: {.callout-note}
   815→## Context % Is Estimated
   816→The monitor uses tool call count as a proxy for context usage since direct access to context window state isn't available. Warnings may appear earlier or later than actual compaction.
   817→:::
   818→
   819→#### Recovery After Compaction
   820→
   821→If compaction happens mid-task, Claude will automatically see:
   822→
   823→1. **Restoration message** --- what plan was active, what task was in progress
   824→2. **Recovery actions** --- read the plan, check git status, continue
   825→
   826→You can also manually point Claude to the right context:
   827→
   828→> "We just had compaction. Read `quality_reports/plans/2026-02-06_translate-lecture5.md` and continue from where we left off."
   829→
   830→---
   831→
   832→# Workflow Patterns {#sec-patterns}
   833→
   834→The first two patterns are **meta-patterns** --- they govern how every task flows. Learn these first, then the specific workflows make more sense.
   835→
   836→## Pattern 1: Plan-First Development {#pattern-1-plan-first-development}
   837→
   838→The plan-first pattern ensures that non-trivial tasks begin with thinking, not typing.
   839→
   840→### Why Planning Matters
   841→
   842→The most common failure mode in AI-assisted development is not bad code --- it is solving the wrong problem, or solving the right problem in a fragile order. Plan-first development forces an explicit design step before any file is touched.
   843→
   844→Without a plan:
   845→
   846→- Claude starts editing immediately, discovers a dependency on slide 3 that changes the approach, and has to undo work
   847→- Context compression discards the reasoning behind a design choice, and Claude makes a contradictory decision later
   848→- The user and Claude have different mental models of what "done" looks like
   849→
   850→With a plan:
   851→
   852→- The approach is agreed upon before any edits happen
   853→- The plan is saved to disk, so it survives compression and session boundaries
   854→- Implementation has a checklist to follow, reducing drift
   855→
   856→### The Protocol
   857→
   858→```
   859→Non-trivial task arrives
   860→  |
   861→  +-- Step 1: Enter Plan Mode (EnterPlanMode)
   862→  +-- Step 2: Draft plan (approach, files, verification)
   863→  +-- Step 3: Save to quality_reports/plans/YYYY-MM-DD_description.md
   864→  +-- Step 4: Present plan to user
   865→  +-- Step 5: User approves (or revises)
   866→  +-- Step 6: Save initial session log (capture context while fresh)
   867→  +-- Step 7: Orchestrator takes over (see Pattern 2)
   868→  +-- Step 8: Update session log + plan status to COMPLETED
   869→```
   870→
   871→### Context Preservation
   872→
   873→Plans are saved to disk specifically so they survive context compression. The rule: **avoid `/clear`** --- prefer auto-compression. Use `/clear` only when context is genuinely polluted.
   874→
   875→For details on how the system automatically preserves and restores context during compaction, see [Context Survival System](#context-survival-system-advanced) in the Building Blocks section.
   876→
   877→### Session Logging
   878→
   879→Session logs (`quality_reports/session_logs/YYYY-MM-DD_description.md`) are a running record of *why* things happened. They have **three distinct behaviors**, each solving a different problem:
   880→
   881→**After plan approval** --- create the log with the goal, plan summary, and rationale for the chosen approach (including rejected alternatives). This captures decisions while context is richest. If you wait, auto-compression may discard the reasoning.
   882→
   883→**During implementation** --- append to the log as you work. Every time a design decision is made, a problem is discovered, or the approach deviates from the plan, write a 1-3 line entry immediately. This is the most important behavior: context gets compressed as the session progresses, and decisions that live only in the conversation will be lost.
   884→
   885→**At session end** --- add a final section with what was accomplished, open questions, and unresolved issues.
   886→
   887→::: {.callout-note}
   888→## Git Records What, Session Logs Record Why
   889→
   890→A commit message says "Update Lecture 5 TikZ diagrams." A session log says "Redesigned the TWFE decomposition diagram because the DA challenge revealed students couldn't trace the path from weights to bias. Considered a table format but chose a flow diagram because it shows directionality."
   891→:::
   892→
   893→::: {.callout-important}
   894→## Why Incremental Logging Matters
   895→
   896→The post-plan log is a special case --- it fires once, right after approval. But the *incremental* logging during implementation is what truly protects you. A 4-hour session that only logs at the start and end loses everything in the middle. Appending decisions as they happen means auto-compression can never erase them --- they are already on disk.
   897→:::
   898→
   899→Claude writes all three log entries automatically --- no need to ask.
   900→
   901→## Pattern 2: Contractor Mode (Orchestrator) {#pattern-2-contractor-mode-orchestrator}
   902→
   903→Once a plan is approved, the orchestrator takes over. It is the natural continuation of Pattern 1: the plan says *what*, the orchestrator handles *how* --- autonomously.
   904→
   905→### The Mental Model
   906→
   907→Think of the orchestrator as a **general contractor**. You are the client. You describe what you want. The plan-first protocol is the blueprint phase. Once you approve the blueprint, the contractor takes over: hires the right specialists (agents), inspects their work (verification), sends them back to fix issues (review-fix loop), and only calls you when the job passes inspection (quality gates).
   908→
   909→### The Loop
   910→
   911→```
   912→User: "Translate Lecture 5 to Quarto"
   913→  |
   914→  |-- Plan-first (Pattern 1): draft plan, save to disk, get approval
   915→  |
   916→  |-- User: "Approved"
   917→  |
   918→  +-- Orchestrator activates:
   919→        |
   920→        Step 1: IMPLEMENT
   921→        |  Execute plan steps (create QMD, translate content, etc.)
   922→        |
   923→        Step 2: VERIFY
   924→        |  Run verifier: render Quarto, check HTML output
   925→        |  If render fails -> fix -> re-render
   926→        |
   927→        Step 3: REVIEW (agents selected by file type)
   928→        |  +--- proofreader ------+
   929→        |  +--- slide-auditor ----+  (parallel)
   930→        |  +--- pedagogy-reviewer +
   931→        |  +--- quarto-critic ----+  (needs others first)
   932→        |
   933→        Step 4: FIX
   934→        |  Apply fixes: Critical -> Major -> Minor
   935→        |  For quarto-critic issues: invoke quarto-fixer
   936→        |
   937→        Step 5: RE-VERIFY
   938→        |  Render again, confirm fixes are clean
   939→        |
   940→        Step 6: SCORE
   941→        |  Apply quality-gates rubric
   942→        |
   943→        +-- Score >= 80?
   944→              YES -> Present summary to user
   945→              NO  -> Loop to Step 3 (max 5 rounds)
   946→```
   947→
   948→### Agent Selection
   949→
   950→The orchestrator selects agents based on which files were touched:
   951→
   952→| Files Modified | Agents Selected |
   953→|---------------|----------------|
   954→| `.tex` only | proofreader + slide-auditor + pedagogy-reviewer |
   955→| `.qmd` only | proofreader + slide-auditor + pedagogy-reviewer |
   956→| `.qmd` with matching `.tex` | Above + quarto-critic (parity check) |
   957→| `.R` scripts | r-reviewer |
   958→| TikZ diagrams present | tikz-reviewer |
   959→| Domain content | domain-reviewer (if configured) |
   960→| Multiple formats | verifier for cross-format parity |
   961→
   962→Agents that are independent of each other run in parallel. The quarto-critic runs after other agents because it may need their context.
   963→
   964→### "Just Do It" Mode
   965→
   966→Sometimes you do not want to approve the final result --- you just want it done:
   967→
   968→> "Translate Lecture 5 to Quarto. Just do it."
   969→
   970→In this mode, the orchestrator still runs the full verify-review-fix loop (quality is non-negotiable), but skips the final approval pause and auto-commits if the score is 80 or above. It still presents the summary so you can see what was done.
   971→
   972→### Relationship to Existing Skills
   973→
   974→The orchestrator does NOT replace skills. It coordinates them:
   975→
   976→- `/qa-quarto` remains available as a standalone adversarial QA loop
   977→- `/slide-excellence` remains available for comprehensive multi-agent review
   978→- `/create-lecture` remains available as a guided creation workflow
   979→
   980→The difference: when you invoke a skill directly, it runs its specific workflow. When the orchestrator is active, it decides which agents to invoke based on context. The orchestrator is the default; skills are for targeted use.
   981→
   982→::: {.callout-tip}
   983→## When to Use Skills vs. the Orchestrator
   984→
   985→**Orchestrator** (automatic): "Translate Lecture 5 to Quarto" --- the orchestrator figures out the agents.
   986→
   987→**Skill** (explicit): "/qa-quarto Lecture5" --- you specifically want the adversarial critic-fixer loop, nothing else.
   988→
   989→Both are valid. The orchestrator is the "I trust you, handle it" path. Skills are the "I know exactly what I want" path.
   990→:::
   991→
   992→## Pattern 3: Creating a New Lecture
   993→
   994→The `/create-lecture` skill guides you through a structured lecture creation workflow --- from gathering source material to deploying polished slides:
   995→
   996→```
   997→/create-lecture
   998→  |
   999→  +-- Phase 1: Gather materials (papers, outlines)
  1000→  +-- Phase 2: Design slide structure
  1001→  +-- Phase 3: Draft Beamer slides
  1002→  +-- Phase 4: Generate R figures
  1003→  +-- Phase 5: Polish and verify
  1004→  |     +-- /slide-excellence (domain + visual + pedagogy)
  1005→  |     +-- /proofread (grammar/typos)
  1006→  |     +-- /visual-audit (layout)
  1007→  +-- Phase 6: Deploy
  1008→        +-- /translate-to-quarto (optional)
  1009→        +-- /deploy
  1010→```
  1011→
  1012→## Pattern 4: Translating Beamer to Quarto
  1013→
  1014→Translation preserves all content while adapting format, converting TikZ to SVG and ggplot to interactive Plotly charts:
  1015→
  1016→```
  1017→/translate-to-quarto Lecture5_Topic.tex
  1018→  |
  1019→  +-- Phase 1-3: Environment mapping + content translation
  1020→  +-- Phase 4-5: Figure conversion (TikZ -> SVG)
  1021→  +-- Phase 6-7: Interactive charts (ggplot -> plotly)
  1022→  +-- Phase 8-9: Render + verify
  1023→  +-- Phase 10-11: /qa-quarto adversarial QA
  1024→        +-- Critic: finds issues
  1025→        +-- Fixer: applies fixes
  1026→        +-- Critic: re-audits
  1027→        +-- ... (until APPROVED or 5 rounds)
  1028→```
  1029→
  1030→## Pattern 5: Replication-First Coding
  1031→
  1032→When working with papers that have replication packages:
  1033→
  1034→```
  1035→Phase 1: Inventory original code
  1036→  +-- Record "gold standard" numbers (Table X, Column Y = Z.ZZ)
  1037→
  1038→Phase 2: Translate (e.g., Stata -> R)
  1039→  +-- Match original specification EXACTLY (same covariates, same clustering)
  1040→
  1041→Phase 3: Verify match
  1042→  +-- Compare every target: paper value vs. our value
  1043→  +-- Tolerance: < 0.01 for estimates, < 0.05 for SEs
  1044→  +-- If mismatch: STOP. Investigate before proceeding.
  1045→
  1046→Phase 4: Only then extend
  1047→  +-- New estimators, new specifications, course-specific figures
  1048→```
  1049→
  1050→::: {.callout-important}
  1051→## Never Skip Replication
  1052→
  1053→In one course, we discovered that a widely-used R package silently produced **incorrect estimates** due to a subtle specification issue. This bug was caught 3 times in different scripts. Without the replication-first protocol, these wrong numbers would have been taught to PhD students.
  1054→:::
  1055→
  1056→## Pattern 6: Multi-Agent Review
  1057→
  1058→The `/slide-excellence` skill runs up to 6 agents in parallel:
  1059→
  1060→```
  1061→/slide-excellence Lecture5_Topic.tex
  1062→  |
  1063→  +-- Agent 1: Visual Audit (slide-auditor)
  1064→  +-- Agent 2: Pedagogical Review (pedagogy-reviewer)
  1065→  +-- Agent 3: Proofreading (proofreader)
  1066→  +-- Agent 4: TikZ Review (tikz-reviewer, if applicable)
  1067→  +-- Agent 5: Content Parity (if Quarto version exists)
  1068→  +-- Agent 6: Substance Review (domain-reviewer)
  1069→  |
  1070→  +-- Synthesize: Combined quality score + prioritized fix list
  1071→```
  1072→
  1073→## Pattern 7: Self-Improvement Loop {#pattern-7-self-improvement-loop}
  1074→
  1075→There are two levels of self-improvement: **quick corrections** via `[LEARN]` tags and **full skill extraction** via `/learn`.
  1076→
  1077→### Quick Corrections: [LEARN] Tags
  1078→
  1079→Every correction gets tagged for future reference in MEMORY.md:
  1080→
  1081→```markdown
  1082→## Corrections Log
  1083→- [LEARN:notation] T_t = 1{t=2} is deterministic -> use T_i in {1,2}
  1084→- [LEARN:citation] Post-LASSO is Belloni (2013), NOT Belloni (2014)
  1085→- [LEARN:r-code] Package X: ALWAYS include intercept in design matrix
  1086→- [LEARN:workflow] Every Beamer edit must auto-sync to Quarto
  1087→```
  1088→
  1089→These tags are searchable and persist across sessions. When Claude encounters a similar situation, it checks memory first.
  1090→
  1091→### Automated Skill Capture: /learn
  1092→
  1093→For discoveries that deserve more than a one-line tag, use `/learn` to create a full skill:
  1094→
  1095→```
  1096→/learn fixest-missing-covariate-handling
  1097→```
  1098→
  1099→The `/learn` skill guides you through a 4-phase workflow:
  1100→
  1101→```
  1102→Phase 1: EVALUATE
  1103→  "Was this non-obvious? Would future-me benefit?"
  1104→  → If YES to any, continue
  1105→         ↓
  1106→Phase 2: CHECK EXISTING
  1107→  Search .claude/skills/ for related skills
  1108→  → Nothing related? Create new. Overlap? Update existing.
  1109→         ↓
  1110→Phase 3: CREATE SKILL
  1111→  Write to .claude/skills/[name]/SKILL.md
  1112→  • Problem statement
  1113→  • Trigger conditions (exact errors, symptoms)
  1114→  • Step-by-step solution
  1115→  • Verification steps
  1116→         ↓
  1117→Phase 4: QUALITY GATE
  1118→  • Description has specific triggers?
  1119→  • Solution verified to work?
  1120→  • Specific enough to be actionable?
  1121→  • General enough to be reusable?
  1122→```
  1123→
  1124→#### When to Use /learn
  1125→
  1126→The context monitor suggests `/learn` at 40%, 55%, and 65% context usage. Consider extracting a skill when you encounter:
  1127→
  1128→| Trigger | Example |
  1129→|---------|---------|
  1130→| Non-obvious debugging | 10+ minute investigation not in docs |
  1131→| Misleading errors | Error message was wrong, found real cause |
  1132→| Workarounds | Found limitation with creative solution |
  1133→| Undocumented APIs | Tool integration not in official docs |
  1134→| Trial-and-error | Multiple attempts before success |
  1135→| Repeatable workflows | Multi-step task you'd do again |
  1136→
  1137→#### Skill vs. [LEARN] Tag
  1138→
  1139→| Situation | Use |
  1140→|-----------|-----|
  1141→| One-liner fix | `[LEARN:category]` tag in MEMORY.md |
  1142→| Multi-step workflow | `/learn` to create full skill |
  1143→| Error + root cause + solution | `/learn` if reusable, `[LEARN]` if not |
  1144→| Package quirk | `/learn` if affects multiple projects |
  1145→
  1146→::: {.callout-tip}
  1147→## Skills Persist, Context Doesn't
  1148→Skills saved to `.claude/skills/` survive compaction and session boundaries. If you discover something valuable late in a session, extract it before compaction erases the details.
  1149→:::
  1150→
  1151→## Pattern 8: Devil's Advocate
  1152→
  1153→At any design decision, invoke the Devil's Advocate:
  1154→
  1155→> "Create a Devil's Advocate. Have it challenge this slide design with 5-7 specific pedagogical questions. Work through each challenge and tell me what survives."
  1156→
  1157→This catches:
  1158→
  1159→- Unstated assumptions
  1160→- Alternative orderings that might work better
  1161→- Notation that could confuse students
  1162→- Missing intuition before formalism
  1163→- Cognitive load issues
  1164→
  1165→## Research Workflows {#sec-research-workflows}
  1166→
  1167→Patterns 1--8 apply to course materials (slides, LaTeX, Quarto). The next three patterns are designed for **research projects** --- papers, simulations, and empirical analysis --- where the rhythm is different: ideas are uncertain, experiments may fail, and code is often written to answer a question rather than to ship.
  1168→
  1169→### Pattern 9: Parallel Agents for Research Tasks
  1170→
  1171→Claude Code can spawn **multiple agents simultaneously** using the Task tool. This is not limited to review --- you can use it for any research or analysis task where independent subtasks can run at the same time.
  1172→
  1173→#### When to Use Parallel Agents
  1174→
  1175→| Scenario | Sequential (slow) | Parallel (fast) |
  1176→|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
  1177→| Reviewing a lecture | Run proofreader, then auditor, then pedagogy | Run all 3 simultaneously |
  1178→| Analyzing 3 papers for a new lecture | Read paper 1, then 2, then 3 | Spawn 3 agents, each reading one paper |
  1179→| Generating figures | Create plot 1, then plot 2, then plot 3 | Spawn agents for independent plots |
  1180→| Comparing estimators | Run simulation 1, then 2, then 3 | Spawn agents for each simulation |
  1181→
  1182→#### How It Works
  1183→
  1184→You do not need to manage this manually. The orchestrator recognizes independent subtasks in a plan and spawns parallel agents automatically --- both during implementation (Step 1) and review (Step 3). For example, if your plan says "read three papers and extract key results," the orchestrator will spawn 3 agents, one per paper, without you asking.
  1185→
  1186→You can also request parallelism explicitly:
  1187→
  1188→> "Read these three papers in parallel. For each, extract the key identification assumption, the main estimator, and whether they have a replication package. Summarize in a table."
  1189→
  1190→Either way, Claude spawns up to 3 Task agents, each processing one paper simultaneously, then synthesizes the results.
  1191→
  1192→#### Practical Limits
  1193→
  1194→- **3 agents** is the sweet spot. More than that increases overhead without proportional speedup.
  1195→- Agents are **independent** --- they cannot see each other's work. If task B depends on task A's output, they must run sequentially.
  1196→- Each agent consumes its own context window. For very large files, sequential processing may be more reliable.
  1197→
  1198→::: {.callout-tip}
  1199→## Cost-Conscious Parallelism
  1200→
  1201→Parallel agents multiply token usage. For cost-sensitive tasks, run the expensive work (Opus agents) sequentially and the cheap work (Sonnet agents) in parallel. The orchestrator already does this: it runs Sonnet-level reviewers in parallel, then the Opus-level critic sequentially.
  1202→:::
  1203→
  1204→### Pattern 10: Research Exploration Workflow {#pattern-10-exploration}
  1205→
  1206→The **exploration workflow** provides a structured sandbox for experimental work.
  1207→
  1208→#### The Problem
  1209→
  1210→Without structure, experimental code scatters across the repository: analysis scripts in `scripts/`, test files in root, comparison documents in `quality_reports/`. After a week of exploration, the repo is cluttered with files that may or may not be useful, and nobody remembers which version was the good one.
  1211→
  1212→#### The Solution: Exploration Folder
  1213→
  1214→All experimental work goes into `explorations/` first:
  1215→
  1216→```
  1217→explorations/
  1218→├── [active-project]/
  1219→│   ├── README.md           # Goal, hypotheses, status
  1220→│   ├── R/                  # Code iterations (_v1, _v2)
  1221→│   ├── scripts/            # Test scripts
  1222→│   └── output/             # Results
  1223→└── ARCHIVE/
  1224→    ├── completed_[name]/   # Graduated to production
  1225→    └── abandoned_[name]/   # Documented why stopped
  1226→```
  1227→
  1228→#### Fast-Track vs. Plan-First
  1229→
  1230→The decision tree is simple:
  1231→
  1232→| Question | Answer | Workflow |
  1233→|----------|--------|----------|
  1234→| "Will this ship?" | YES | Plan-First (80/100 quality) |
  1235→| "Am I testing an idea?" | YES | Fast-Track (60/100 quality) |
  1236→| "Does this improve the project?" | NO | Don't build it |
  1237→
  1238→Fast-Track explorations skip formal planning. Instead, a 2-minute **research value check** gates the work: "Does this improve the paper/slides/analysis?" If the answer is "maybe", explore. If "no", skip. If "yes", use Plan-First rigor.
  1239→
  1240→#### The Lifecycle
  1241→
  1242→```
  1243→Research value check (2 min)
  1244→  ↓
  1245→Create explorations/[project]/ (5 min)
  1246→  ↓
  1247→Code without overhead (60/100 quality)
  1248→  ↓
  1249→Decision point (1-2 hours):
  1250→  ├── Graduate → Move to R/, scripts/, tests/ (upgrade to 80/100)
  1251→  ├── Keep exploring → Stay in explorations/
  1252→  └── Abandon → Archive with brief explanation
  1253→```
  1254→
  1255→The **kill switch** is explicit: at any point, you can stop, archive with a one-paragraph explanation, and move on. No guilt, no sunk cost. See `.claude/rules/exploration-folder-protocol.md` and `.claude/rules/exploration-fast-track.md` for the full protocols.
  1256→
  1257→#### Simplified Orchestrator for Research
  1258→
  1259→The full orchestrator (Pattern 2) is designed for course materials with multi-agent review loops. For research projects, the **simple variant** strips this down to: implement → verify → score → done. No multi-round reviews, no parallel agent spawning. This lives in its own path-scoped rule (`.claude/rules/orchestrator-research.md`) that loads only when working on R scripts or explorations.
  1260→
  1261→#### Merge-Only Quality Reporting
  1262→
  1263→In research projects, commits are frequent and incremental. Generating a quality report for each commit creates noise. Instead, quality reports are generated **only at merge time** --- a permanent snapshot of what was merged and why. Session logs capture the ongoing reasoning. See `.claude/rules/session-logging.md`.
  1264→
  1265→### Pattern 11: Research Skills {#pattern-11-research-skills}
  1266→
  1267→Five skills support the research workflow beyond slide development:
  1268→
  1269→| Skill | What It Does | When to Use |
  1270→|-------|-------------|-------------|
  1271→| `/lit-review [topic]` | Search, synthesize, and identify gaps in the literature | Starting a new project or section |
  1272→| `/research-ideation [topic]` | Generate research questions, hypotheses, and empirical strategies | Brainstorming phase |
  1273→| `/interview-me [topic]` | Interactive interview to formalize a vague idea into a concrete specification | When you have an intuition but not a plan |
  1274→| `/review-paper [file]` | Full manuscript review with referee objections | Before submission or after a draft |
  1275→| `/data-analysis [data]` | End-to-end R analysis: explore, regress, produce publication-ready output | Empirical analysis phase |
  1276→
  1277→These skills produce structured reports saved to `quality_reports/`. The `/data-analysis` skill also generates R scripts (saved to `scripts/R/`) and runs the r-reviewer agent automatically.
  1278→
  1279→::: {.callout-tip}
  1280→## Research Workflow Sequence
  1281→A typical research project might flow: `/research-ideation` (what to study?) → `/interview-me` (formalize the idea) → `/lit-review` (what's been done?) → `/data-analysis` (run the analysis) → `/review-paper` (check the draft).
  1282→:::
  1283→
  1284→### Pattern 12: Branch Isolation with Git Worktrees (Advanced) {#pattern-12-branch-isolation}
  1285→
  1286→::: {.callout-note}
  1287→## Advanced Pattern
  1288→This pattern is optional and primarily useful for major translations, risky refactors, or multi-day projects. Most day-to-day work doesn't need it.
  1289→:::
  1290→
  1291→Git worktrees create a **separate working directory** linked to the same repository. Each directory has its own branch but shares commit history.
  1292→
  1293→```
  1294→your-project/                     ← main branch (stays clean)
  1295→.worktrees/lecture-06-quarto/     ← isolated branch (Claude works here)
  1296→```
  1297→
  1298→#### Why Use Worktrees?
  1299→
  1300→| Benefit | Example |
  1301→|---------|---------|
  1302→| **Safe experimentation** | Translate Lecture 6 to Quarto --- if it fails, main is untouched |
  1303→| **Clean history** | 50 intermediate commits squash into one clean commit |
  1304→| **Easy discard** | Wrong approach? Delete worktree, no trace in main |
  1305→| **Multi-session work** | Resume worktree next day, no context loss |
  1306→| **Parallel work** | Work on slides (main) while Claude translates (worktree) |
  1307→
  1308→#### The Workflow
  1309→
  1310→```
  1311→1. CREATE WORKTREE
  1312→   git worktree add .worktrees/lecture-06-quarto -b quarto/lecture-06
  1313→   cd .worktrees/lecture-06-quarto
  1314→         ↓
  1315→2. IMPLEMENT
  1316→   All changes happen in the worktree
  1317→   Commit frequently (intermediate commits are OK)
  1318→         ↓
  1319→3. VERIFY
  1320→   Run tests, render, review against worktree only
  1321→         ↓
  1322→4. SYNC TO MAIN (when ready)
  1323→   git checkout main
  1324→   git merge --squash quarto/lecture-06
  1325→   git commit -m "feat: add Lecture 6 Quarto version"
  1326→         ↓
  1327→5. CLEANUP
  1328→   git worktree remove .worktrees/lecture-06-quarto
  1329→   git branch -d quarto/lecture-06
  1330→```
  1331→
  1332→#### Commands Reference
  1333→
  1334→```bash
  1335→# Create a worktree with new branch
  1336→git worktree add .worktrees/[name] -b [branch-name]
  1337→
  1338→# List active worktrees
  1339→git worktree list
  1340→
  1341→# Remove a worktree (after merging or abandoning)
  1342→git worktree remove .worktrees/[name]
  1343→
  1344→# Delete the branch (after removal)
  1345→git branch -d [branch-name]
  1346→
  1347→# Squash-merge into main
  1348→git checkout main
  1349→git merge --squash [branch-name]
  1350→git commit -m "feat: description of changes"
  1351→```
  1352→
  1353→#### When to Use
  1354→
  1355→| Situation | Use Worktree? |
  1356→|-----------|---------------|
  1357→| Quick fix to one file | No --- just edit main |
  1358→| New lecture creation | Maybe --- if multi-session |
  1359→| Beamer → Quarto translation | Yes --- many intermediate states |
  1360→| Major refactor | Yes --- safe rollback |
  1361→| Experimenting with new approach | Yes --- easy discard |
  1362→
  1363→#### Complexity Cost
  1364→
  1365→- Adds ~3 commands to learn
  1366→- Adds mental model: "Where am I working?"
  1367→- Requires discipline to sync/discard, not leave orphan worktrees
  1368→
  1369→For most novice users, working directly on main with frequent commits is simpler and sufficient. Use worktrees when the benefits of isolation outweigh the added complexity.
  1370→
  1371→---
  1372→
  1373→# Customizing for Your Domain {#sec-customize}
  1374→
  1375→## Step 1: Build Your Knowledge Base
  1376→
  1377→The knowledge base (`.claude/rules/knowledge-base-template.md`) is the most domain-specific component. It provides skeleton tables for notation conventions, lecture progression, applications, design principles, anti-patterns, and R code pitfalls. Fill them in as you develop your project --- you don't need everything upfront.
  1378→
  1379→### Notation Registry
  1380→
  1381→```markdown
  1382→| Symbol | Meaning | Introduced | Anti-Pattern |
  1383→|--------|---------|------------|-------------|
  1384→| $\beta$ | Regression coefficient | Lecture 1 | Don't use $b$ |
  1385→| $\hat{\theta}$ | Estimator | Lecture 2 | Don't use $\hat{\beta}$ for different estimand |
  1386→```
  1387→
  1388→### Applications Database
  1389→
  1390→```markdown
  1391→| Application | Paper | Dataset | Package | Lecture |
  1392→|------------|-------|---------|---------|--------|
  1393→| Minimum Wage | Card & Krueger (1994) | NJ/PA fast food | `fixest` | 3 |
  1394→```
  1395→
  1396→### Validated Design Principles
  1397→
  1398→```markdown
  1399→| Principle | Evidence | Lectures Applied |
  1400→|-----------|----------|-----------------|
  1401→| Motivation before formalism | DA challenge: "students lost" | All |
  1402→| Max 3 new symbols per slide | Pedagogy review caught overload | 2, 4 |
  1403→```
  1404→
  1405→## Step 2: Create Your Domain Reviewer
  1406→
  1407→Copy `.claude/agents/domain-reviewer.md` and customize the 5 lenses for your field. The template provides the structure; you fill in domain-specific checks.
  1408→
  1409→## Step 3: Adapt Your Theme
  1410→
  1411→The template includes an example Quarto theme SCSS file. To customize:
  1412→
  1413→1. Change the color palette to your institution's colors
  1414→2. Update CSS class names if needed
  1415→3. Modify the beamer-translator environment mapping to match your classes
  1416→
  1417→## Step 4: Creating Custom Skills {#sec-create-skills}
  1418→
  1419→The guide includes 19 skills for common academic tasks. But if you have repetitive workflows specific to your domain, you can create your own.
  1420→
  1421→### When to Create a Skill
  1422→
  1423→Create a skill when:
  1424→- You repeatedly explain the same 3+ step workflow to Claude
  1425→- You need domain-specific quality checks (citation style, notation consistency, lab protocols)
  1426→- You enforce field-specific output formats (thesis structure, journal templates)
  1427→- You coordinate multi-tool workflows (data → analysis → manuscript)
  1428→
  1429→**Don't create a skill for:**
  1430→- One-time tasks
  1431→- Workflows that change frequently
  1432→- Simple 1-2 step operations
  1433→
  1434→### Skill Structure
  1435→
  1436→Each skill is a directory in `.claude/skills/` with a `SKILL.md` file:
  1437→
  1438→```markdown
  1439→---
  1440→name: your-skill-name
  1441→description: [What it does] + [When to use] + [Key capabilities]
  1442→argument-hint: "[brief hint for user]"
  1443→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Edit", "Bash", "Task"]
  1444→---
  1445→
  1446→# Your Skill Name
  1447→
  1448→## Instructions
  1449→Step 1: [First action with details]
  1450→Step 2: [Second action]
  1451→...
  1452→
  1453→## Examples
  1454→Example 1: [Common scenario]
  1455→...
  1456→
  1457→## Troubleshooting
  1458→Error: [Common error]
  1459→Solution: [How to fix]
  1460→```
  1461→
  1462→### Writing Effective Trigger Descriptions
  1463→
  1464→The `description` field determines when Claude loads your skill. Use specific trigger phrases users would actually say:
  1465→
  1466→**Good (Citation Style Enforcement):**
  1467→```yaml
  1468→description: Enforces APA 7th edition citation format. Use when user asks to "check citations", "fix references", "apply APA style", or when reviewing .tex/.qmd files with bibliographies.
  1469→```
  1470→
  1471→**Good (Lab Notebook Entry):**
  1472→```yaml
  1473→description: Generates structured lab notebook entries from experimental notes. Use when user provides "experiment notes", "protocol results", or asks to "format lab entry".
  1474→```
  1475→
  1476→**Bad (Too Vague):**
  1477→```yaml
  1478→description: Helps with citations
  1479→```
  1480→
  1481→### Domain-Specific Examples
  1482→
  1483→::: {.panel-tabset}
  1484→

  1517→### Quick Start
  1518→
  1519→1. **Copy the template:**
  1520→   ```bash
  1521→   cp templates/skill-template.md .claude/skills/your-skill-name/SKILL.md
  1522→   ```
  1523→
  1524→2. **Customize for your domain:**
  1525→   - Replace trigger phrases with your field's terminology
  1526→   - Add domain-specific file types and tools
  1527→   - Include field conventions and common errors
  1528→
  1529→3. **Test the skill:**
  1530→   - Restart Claude Code or type `/reload`
  1531→   - Use one of your trigger phrases
  1532→   - Verify the skill loads and produces correct output
  1533→
  1534→4. **Iterate:**
  1535→   - If skill doesn't trigger: Revise description with more specific phrases
  1536→   - If instructions unclear: Add more examples
  1537→   - If output wrong: Add validation steps
  1538→
  1539→**Full template:** See `templates/skill-template.md` for comprehensive examples from biology, economics, and physics.
  1540→
  1541→## Tips from 6+ Sessions of Iteration
  1542→
  1543→1. **Keep CLAUDE.md under 150 lines.** Claude follows ~150 instructions reliably. A 400-line CLAUDE.md means rules get silently ignored. Use path-scoped rules for detailed standards.
  1544→2. **Add rules incrementally.** Don't try to write all rules upfront. Add them when you discover patterns. Use `paths:` frontmatter so they only load when relevant.
  1545→3. **Use the [LEARN] format.** Every correction gets tagged and persisted in MEMORY.md. This prevents repeating mistakes across sessions.
  1546→4. **Trust the adversarial pattern.** The critic-fixer loop catches things you won't. Let it run.
  1547→5. **Verify everything.** The verification rule exists for a reason. Never skip compilation or rendering checks.
  1548→6. **Session logs matter.** Document design decisions, not just what changed. Future-you will thank present-you.
  1549→7. **Devil's Advocate early.** Challenge slide structure before you've built 50 slides on a shaky foundation.
  1550→8. **Progressive disclosure.** Start with CLAUDE.md + 2--3 rules. Add more as your workflow matures. Newcomers should not face 17 rules on day one.

  1638→## Troubleshooting
  1639→
  1640→### LaTeX Won't Compile
  1641→
  1642→**Symptom:** `xelatex` errors or missing packages.
  1643→
  1644→**Fix:**
  1645→1. Check you have XeLaTeX installed: `which xelatex`
  1646→2. Ensure `TEXINPUTS` includes `Preambles/`: the `/compile-latex` skill handles this
  1647→3. Missing package? Install via TeX Live: `tlmgr install [package]`
  1648→
  1649→### Quarto Won't Render
  1650→
  1651→**Symptom:** `quarto render` fails or produces broken HTML.
  1652→
  1653→**Fix:**
  1654→1. Check Quarto version: `quarto --version` (need 1.3+)
  1655→2. Check for syntax errors in YAML frontmatter
  1656→3. Missing TikZ SVGs? Run `/extract-tikz` first
  1657→
  1658→### Hooks Not Firing
  1659→
  1660→**Symptom:** No context warnings, no verification reminders.
  1661→
  1662→**Fix:**
  1663→1. Check hooks are configured: `cat .claude/settings.json | grep hooks`
  1664→2. Ensure Python 3 is available: `which python3`
  1665→3. Check hook file permissions: `ls -la .claude/hooks/`
  1666→
  1667→### Claude Ignores Rules
  1668→
  1669→**Symptom:** Claude doesn't follow conventions in `.claude/rules/`.
  1670→
  1671→**Fix:**
  1672→1. Rules use `paths:` frontmatter — check the path matches your files
  1673→2. Too many rules? Claude follows ~150 instructions reliably. Consolidate.
  1674→3. Try: *"Read `.claude/rules/[rule].md` and follow it for this task"*
  1675→
  1676→### Context Lost After Compaction
  1677→
  1678→**Symptom:** Claude forgets what you were working on.
  1679→
  1680→**Fix:**
  1681→1. Point Claude to the plan: *"Read `quality_reports/plans/[latest].md`"*
  1682→2. Check session log: *"Read `quality_reports/session_logs/[latest].md`"*
  1683→3. The `post-compact-restore.py` hook should print recovery info automatically
  1684→
  1685→### Quality Score Too Low
  1686→
  1687→**Symptom:** Score stuck below 80, can't commit.
  1688→
  1689→**Fix:**
  1690→1. Run `/slide-excellence` to get detailed issue breakdown
  1691→2. Fix critical issues first (they cost -10 to -20 points each)
  1692→3. Ask Claude: *"What are the remaining critical issues?"*
  1693→
  1694→### Skills Not Auto-Invoked
  1695→
  1696→**Symptom:** Claude doesn't use skills when you describe a task.
  1697→
  1698→**Fix:**
  1699→1. Be explicit in your request: *"Review my slides for grammar and layout issues"*
  1700→2. Check skill has auto-invocation enabled (no `disable-model-invocation: true`)
  1701→3. Skill descriptions help Claude know when to use them — check they're clear
